era_context: The early 14th century was marked by the fragmentation of Mongol unity, the consolidation of European feudalism, and the rise of maritime trade networks. The Yuan Dynasty was experiencing internal factional conflict; the Mamluk Sultanate dominated the Eastern Mediterranean; the Papacy was in Avignon; and the Scottish Wars of Independence were in their final phases. This was a period of relative political instability and religious reform.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources beyond Mongol chronicles)

events.title: Council of Vienne convened by Pope Clement V
events.description: Pope Clement V convened the Council of Vienne in October 1311 to address major issues facing the Church, including the suppression of the Knights Templar, the organization of crusades, and Church reform. The council was heavily influenced by King Philip IV of France, who sought the suppression of the Templar order. The council would continue through 1313 and would formally dissolve the Templars and transfer their properties to the Knights Hospitaller. The council represented a major assertion of secular royal power over the Church.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay, Grand Master of the Templars

sources.name: Papal bulls and council records (primary documents)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Vienne's convocation in October 1311 is well-documented in papal records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Led to 1313-001 (Council concludes and Templars formally dissolved)

events.title: Timur Khan becomes regent of Yuan Dynasty
events.description: Timur Khan (also known as Temür Khan) became regent of the Yuan Dynasty, consolidating power after the death of the previous regent. Timur Khan's regency marked a period of relative stability in Yuan politics, though factional conflict among Mongol princes continued. His regency would last until his death in 1314, after which Ayurbarwada would assume full power.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Timur Khan's regency is attested in Yuan records, though the exact chronology is somewhat unclear. The date 1311 is conventional but may be approximate (±1 year).
cross_references: Led to 1314-005 (Ayurbarwada assumes full power after Timur Khan's death)

events.title: Robert the Bruce defeats English forces at Bannockburn (preliminary skirmishes)
events.description: In the lead-up to the major Battle of Bannockburn in 1314, Robert the Bruce and his Scottish forces engaged in preliminary military campaigns against English forces in Scotland. These skirmishes and sieges were part of the broader Scottish Wars of Independence and set the stage for the decisive battle of 1314. Bruce's military successes in 1311–1313 consolidated his control of Scotland and weakened English positions.
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce), King of Scotland

sources.name: Barbour, John. The Brus (compiled early 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Scottish military campaigns in 1311–1313 are attested in contemporary chronicles, but specific dates and details of individual skirmishes are often uncertain. The broader pattern of Scottish military success is clear.
cross_references: Part of broader Scottish Wars of Independence (1296–1357)

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate consolidates control of the Levant
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad consolidated its control of the Levant and Egypt, establishing itself as the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean. The Mamluks had defeated the Mongols at the Battle of Ain Jalut in 1260 and had subsequently expanded their territory. By 1311, the Mamluk Sultanate was at the height of its power and influence, controlling major trade routes and serving as a major patron of Islamic culture and architecture.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (Mamluk chronicle, compiled 15th century)
Ibn al-Athir and other Arabic chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Mamluk Sultanate's consolidation of power is attested in Arabic chronicles, but the specific date of 1311 is approximate. The broader pattern of Mamluk expansion and consolidation is clear.

events.title: Clement V issues papal bull Pastoralis praeeminentia
events.description: Pope Clement V issued the papal bull Pastoralis praeeminentia, which addressed the suppression of the Knights Templar and the transfer of their properties to the Knights Hospitaller. The bull was part of the Council of Vienne's proceedings and represented a major assertion of papal authority over the Templar order. The bull also addressed other ecclesiastical matters and Church reform.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay, Grand Master of the Templars

sources.name: Papal bulls and council records (primary documents)

events.certainty_note: The papal bull Pastoralis praeeminentia is well-documented in papal records. The date is secure.
cross_references: Led to 1313-001 (Council concludes and Templars formally dissolved)

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Vienne's significance is sometimes overstated; while it formally addressed the Templar question, the suppression had already been largely accomplished through papal pressure and royal force before the council convened. Timur Khan's regency is attested in Yuan records, but the exact chronology and his relationship to Ayurbarwada are debated by scholars. The preliminary Scottish military campaigns of 1311–1313 are less well-documented than the Battle of Bannockburn itself; specific dates and details are often uncertain. The Mamluk Sultanate's consolidation of power was a gradual process rather than a discrete event in 1311.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1311 is strong for Western Europe (France, the Papacy) due to abundant monastic chronicles and papal records. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) are detailed but compiled retrospectively. Arabic sources on the Mamluk Sultanate are detailed but compiled retrospectively. Scottish sources are limited to English chronicles and later Scottish compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Indian subcontinent are poorly represented in surviving written records. Bias toward political and religious events over economic and social history is inherent in the surviving sources. The Council of Vienne's proceedings are well-documented, but the council's other decisions and reforms are less well-remembered.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Vienne (1311–1313) continued through 1312 and formally dissolved the Templars in 1313.
Timur Khan's regency led to Ayurbarwada's co-regency in 1312.
Scottish military campaigns in 1311–1313 led to the decisive Battle of Bannockburn in 1314.
The papal bull was part of the Council of Vienne's proceedings.
